column1: # This is not legal advice. Research-grade precheck output (schema 1.0); see compliance_matrix.md for context.

column3: Mission profile schema validation (stdlib manual validation)
column9: all required fields present with expected types
column14: Manual validation dual-maintained with the schema document; unknown fields are ignored by every rule and can never produce a PASS.

column3: National authorization and continuing supervision of the mission
column5: States Parties to the Outer Space Treaty
column9: rule does not apply to this profile
column10: Outer Space Treaty, Article VI (610 UNTS 205)
column14: Precheck only: verifies that authorization evidence is DECLARED, not that a valid authorization exists or covers this activity. Which state is the 'appropriate State Party' for a multi-state mission requires legal review.

column3: Registry-state jurisdiction and control: owner consent for active interference
column5: States Parties to the Outer Space Treaty
column10: Outer Space Treaty, Article VIII (610 UNTS 205)
column14: The consent requirement is an INTERPRETATION derived from Art. VIII's allocation of jurisdiction and control - it is not express treaty text, and its precise legal basis requires legal review. For research profiles, consent 'true' is a declared scenario assumption (ADSC spec D9), never a legal fact, and the finding notes must be read that way. The consent instrument (agreement, contract, note verbale) required in a real case also needs legal review.

column3: Registration of the servicer space object
column5: States Parties to the Registration Convention
column9: rule does not apply to this profile
column10: Convention on Registration of Objects Launched into Outer Space, Article II (1023 UNTS 15)
column14: Precheck of declared evidence only. Registration timing ('as soon as practicable') and the choice of registry state among joint launching states require legal review.

column5: States Parties to the Liability Convention
column9: rule does not apply to this profile
column10: Convention on International Liability for Damage Caused by Space Objects, Articles II-III (961 UNTS 187)
column14: Placeholder: the convention binds STATES, not operators - the operator-level insurance/indemnification check performed here is NOT imposed by the cited instrument itself but by national law, which varies by jurisdiction. binding_type is therefore 'placeholder' (conservatism policy); amounts and scope require legal review.

column3: Post-mission disposal lifetime within 25 years (IADC guideline)
column10: IADC Space Debris Mitigation Guidelines, IADC-02-01, guideline 5.3.2 (objects passing through the LEO protected region)
column11: IADC-02-01 Rev. 2 (March 2020); later revisions exist - confirm the current one
column14: Non-binding guideline. Stricter national rules supersede it where they apply - notably the US FCC 5-year rule (see FCC-ODM-5YR); under a US FCC-jurisdiction scenario the 25-year figure is NOT the operative standard. Confirm the current IADC revision before citing.

column3: Post-mission disposal within 5 years for LEO space stations (FCC-jurisdiction scenario)
column9: rule does not apply to this profile
column10: FCC, Second Report and Order, 'Mitigation of Orbital Debris in the New Space Age', FCC 22-74 (adopted 29 September 2022); 47 CFR Part 25 space-station disposal rules
column14: Simplified jurisdiction test (operating_state/launching_state == 'US'); the real trigger is an FCC space-station authorization, including US market access for non-US systems. Exact codified CFR subsections, transition periods and waiver practice require legal review.

column3: Orbital debris mitigation disclosure with the FCC application
column9: rule does not apply to this profile
column10: 47 CFR 25.114(d)(14) (orbital debris mitigation disclosure for space station applications)
column14: Precheck of declared evidence only; the disclosure's technical content (collision risk, casualty risk, maneuverability, trackability) is not evaluated here. Requires legal review.

column3: FAA launch and reentry authorization evidence (US commercial scenario)
column5: Federal Aviation Administration, Office of Commercial Space Transportation
column9: rule does not apply to this profile
column10: 51 U.S.C. chapter 509 (Commercial Space Launch Activities); 14 CFR Part 450 (Launch and Reentry License Requirements)
column14: Precheck of declared evidence only. Whether the deorbit-kit-driven target reentry itself needs a Part 450 reentry authorization (vs. only the servicer launch) is an open legal question for ADR concepts - requires legal review.

column3: NOAA private remote-sensing system authorization evidence
column5: National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration, Commercial Remote Sensing Regulatory Affairs
column9: rule does not apply to this profile
column10: 51 U.S.C. chapter 601 (Land Remote Sensing Policy); 15 CFR Part 960 (Licensing of Private Remote Sensing Space Systems)
column14: Placeholder rule: the US-nexus test (US person / platform control) and the 2020 tiering system are NOT modelled - the rule fires on any remote-sensing payload. Rendezvous inspection cameras may or may not fall under Part 960. Requires legal review.

column3: Frequency coordination / notification filing evidence for the RF payload
column5: International Telecommunication Union (via the responsible national administration)
column9: rule does not apply to this profile
column10: ITU Radio Regulations, Article 9 (coordination) and Article 11 (notification and recording)
column11: Radio Regulations, Edition of 2020; later editions exist - confirm the current one
column14: Placeholder rule: ITU obligations run through the responsible national administration, and band-specific coordination requirements are NOT modelled. The Radio Regulations bind administrations, not operators directly. Requires review by the responsible administration / legal counsel.

column3: ESA space debris mitigation: LEO clearance reference (5-year class)
column9: rule does not apply to this profile
column10: ESA Space Debris Mitigation Requirements, ESSB-ST-U-007, Issue 1 (30 October 2023)
column14: ESSB-ST-U-007 applies contractually to ESA-procured missions; it is cited here as a reference for where European practice is heading, not as a legal requirement on third parties. Clause-level applicability requires review.

column5: European Space Agency and Zero Debris community
column9: No Zero Debris alignment statement is declared (informational: the charter is voluntary; an ADR evidence package benefits from stating its position).; evidence_documents: does not contain 'zero_debris_alignment_statement'
column14: Voluntary charter, not a legal requirement. Informational finding only.

column3: No unconsented active interference with a registered space object (ADSC policy)
column10: ADSC specification v4.2, decisions D9 and D11 (adsc-specification-v4.md)
column14: Internal policy, stricter than a legal minimum by design. For research profiles the consent flag is a declared scenario assumption (D9).

column3: No live-ephemeris ingestion and no target-specific operational products (ADSC policy)
column10: ADSC specification v4.2, decision D11 and hard rule R13 (adsc-specification-v4.md)
column14: Internal policy. Either flag alone fails the rule; a missing flag evaluates UNKNOWN, never PASS.

column3: Non-weaponization: research-only or consented, class-level use (ADSC policy)
column10: ADSC specification v4.2, section 1 and decision D11; README peaceful-use disclaimer
column14: Proxy check: 'non-weaponization' is not directly observable from profile fields; the rule uses research-only / consent / no-targeting-products as the observable proxy. Internal policy, not law.

column5: ADSC project policy (referencing US ITAR/EAR as the typical regime)
column9: Export-control review is not complete. Open-literature methods (spec D11) are the current basis for research distribution, but a documented review is required before sharing controlled technical data or starting hardware/flight work.
column11: snapshot 2024-era regime structure; regimes change - re-verify
column14: Placeholder: jurisdiction-specific export regimes (US ITAR/EAR cited as the typical case) are not modelled in detail. Requires export-control counsel review.

column3: WP5 campaign compliance metadata consistent with this mission profile
column9: WP5 campaign metadata consistent with research profile; all 7 mapped columns + 2 policy columns consistent across 1000 runs
column10: generated/wp5_campaign_runs.csv compliance columns (schema 1.0); ADSC specification v4.2, D9/D11
column14: Consistency check between two research artifacts; it makes no statement about the real world. If the WP5 CSV is absent the result is UNKNOWN, never PASS.
